content:
5 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Every few months, the AI industry announces another “largest language model ever built.” More parameters, more GPUs, more data, more benchmarks. The implicit assumption seems obvious: if we continue making language models larger, artificial intelligence will naturally become better. But what if we are optimizing the wrong thing? After watching the rapid evolution of ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, DeepSeek, and many other AI assistants, I keep coming back to a simple question:
Does every AI task really need a giant language model?
The answer, in my opinion, is no. Many of the tasks we perform every day, i.e., rewriting an email, summarizing a document, formatting text, solving simple mathematics, retrieving information, or searching the web, do not require a model that has memorized nearly the entire internet. Yet today’s AI assistants typically activate enormous models regardless of whether the question is trivial or highly complex.

Humans Don’t Work Like Large Language Models
One of the inspirations behind my recent concept paper comes from human cognition. No human being knows everything. A physician is not expected to be an expert in compiler optimization. A software engineer is not expected to remember every chemistry equation. A lawyer does not memorize the complete medical literature. Instead, expertise is distributed. Even inside our own brains, memory and reasoning are handled by different systems. We retrieve knowledge when needed, reason over it, and update our understanding continuously.
Current LLMs work very differently.
Knowledge, reasoning, language understanding, and task execution are all compressed into a single gigantic neural network. The model must simultaneously remember facts, understand language, reason, generate code, translate languages, solve mathematics, maintain conversations, and perform hundreds of unrelated tasks. This monolithic design has undoubtedly been successful, but perhaps it is not the only path forward.

What If AI Worked Like an Operating System?
Instead of relying on one massive model, imagine AI functioning more like a modern operating system. The operating system itself is relatively lightweight. Whenever a specific task is required, it loads only the appropriate application. Your computer doesn’t launch Photoshop every time you open a text file. It launches only what is necessary. Why shouldn’t AI behave similarly? This idea forms the foundation of the modular architecture proposed in my concept paper. Rather than building one gigantic model responsible for everything, the system is divided into three cooperating components.
1. A Unified Core Module
The first component is a compact reasoning and orchestration model. Its responsibilities are intentionally limited. It understands language. It detects user intent. It interprets emotion and conversational context. It performs safety checks. It decides which external tool or expert should handle the task. Instead of trying to become an encyclopedia of human knowledge, its primary responsibility is intelligent coordination.
2. A Cognitive Memory Module
Current language models typically store knowledge inside billions of learned parameters. That creates a fundamental limitation. Knowledge eventually becomes outdated. Instead, I propose separating memory from reasoning. A dedicated Cognitive Memory Module would manage different categories of information: working memory, episodic memory, cached information, static knowledge, dynamic knowledge. The memory system could continuously retrieve, organize, update, and discard information without retraining the language model itself. More importantly, this memory could remain under the user’s control, enabling privacy-preserving and personalized AI systems.
3. Specialized Expert Models
Instead of asking one model to master every discipline, the architecture employs independent expert models. Examples include: Mathematics, Programming, Physics, Medicine, Finance, Translation, Search, Scientific literature retrieval, Web crawling, Document analysis. Only the experts required for a particular task are activated. If the user needs mathematical reasoning, only the mathematics expert participates. If they need programming assistance, only the programming expert is involved. The remaining experts remain idle. This dramatically reduces unnecessary computation.

Bigger Isn’t Always Better
Recent advances already hint that this direction may be practical. Sparse Mixture-of-Experts architectures activate only a fraction of their parameters for each input. Tool-using language models increasingly rely on calculators, search engines, APIs, and external software instead of solving everything internally. Small language models continue to achieve impressive performance on carefully targeted tasks. Taken together, these developments suggest a broader trend:
Intelligence may come less from making models larger and more from organizing them more effectively.

Sustainability Matters
One motivation behind this work is sustainability. Training and operating frontier AI systems require substantial energy, specialized hardware, and cooling infrastructure. As AI becomes integrated into billions of daily interactions, efficiency becomes increasingly important. If a specialized model can solve a problem using only a fraction of the computation of a frontier model, then using the larger model simply because it exists may not always be the most responsible engineering decision. The future of AI should not be measured solely by benchmark scores. It should also consider energy efficiency, accessibility, deployment cost, and environmental impact.

Personal AI Instead of Universal AI
Another exciting possibility is personalization. Imagine owning your own collection of AI experts. A medical researcher could install biology and genomics experts. An accountant could maintain tax and auditing experts. A software engineer could customize programming experts. A university could deploy educational experts. Most importantly, these experts could learn locally from user feedback without affecting the global model shared by everyone else. Instead of one universal AI attempting to satisfy every possible user, we could have highly personalized AI ecosystems.

A New Software Industry?
If this vision becomes reality, it may fundamentally change software development. Today, only a handful of organizations possess the resources required to build frontier-scale language models. A modular ecosystem could democratize AI development. Universities could publish specialized research experts. Hospitals could build medical experts. Law firms could develop legal experts. Startups could create domain-specific models without needing to compete with trillion-dollar foundation model companies. AI models themselves could become reusable software components, much like software libraries are today.

This Is a Beginning, Not a Conclusion
The architecture described here is intentionally conceptual. It has not yet been implemented or experimentally validated. Many important research questions remain: How should queries be routed? How should expert boundaries be defined? How should memory be organized? How should efficiency be measured? How much computation can actually be saved? These questions require careful empirical investigation. Nevertheless, I believe the conversation itself is important. For years, AI progress has largely been driven by one equation: More parameters = Better AI. Perhaps the next generation of AI will follow a different equation: Better architecture/ System Design= Better AI.
Instead of asking how to build larger models, perhaps we should start asking how to build smarter systems. That shift, from scaling models to designing intelligent architectures, may ultimately prove to be the more sustainable path toward artificial intelligence.
